verses.text: Solomon begins the temple in the four hundred and eightieth year after the Exodus — dating God's house to the redemption that made Israel a people. The measurements are exact and modest. What is built for God is shaped to His design, not merely to human grandeur.

Solomon begins the temple in the four hundred and eightieth year after the Exodus — dating God's house to the redemption that made Israel a people. The measurements are exact and modest. What is built for God is shaped to His design, not merely to human grandeur.

Solomon begins the temple in the four hundred and eightieth year after the Exodus — dating God's house to the redemption that made Israel a people. The measurements are exact and modest. What is built for God is shaped to His design, not merely to human grandeur.

Chambers ring the house, and the great stones are dressed at the quarry, so 'there was neither hammer nor axe... heard' as it rose. The holy place went up in silence. God's truest building is quiet and unforced — the work prepared beforehand and set in place without strife.

Chambers ring the house, and the great stones are dressed at the quarry, so 'there was neither hammer nor axe... heard' as it rose. The holy place went up in silence. God's truest building is quiet and unforced — the work prepared beforehand and set in place without strife.

Chambers ring the house, and the great stones are dressed at the quarry, so 'there was neither hammer nor axe... heard' as it rose. The holy place went up in silence. God's truest building is quiet and unforced — the work prepared beforehand and set in place without strife.

Side chambers and winding stairs are fitted around the temple, every part planned and joined. Nothing is haphazard in the house of God. He cares about structure and order; the details others would overlook are not beneath His notice or His glory.

Side chambers and winding stairs are fitted around the temple, every part planned and joined. Nothing is haphazard in the house of God. He cares about structure and order; the details others would overlook are not beneath His notice or His glory.

As the work rises, God's word comes: if Solomon walks in His statutes, 'I will dwell among the children of Israel, and will not forsake my people.' The promise is greater than the building — God's presence, on condition of obedience. He desires not a house only, but a faithful people.

As the work rises, God's word comes: if Solomon walks in His statutes, 'I will dwell among the children of Israel, and will not forsake my people.' The promise is greater than the building — God's presence, on condition of obedience. He desires not a house only, but a faithful people.

As the work rises, God's word comes: if Solomon walks in His statutes, 'I will dwell among the children of Israel, and will not forsake my people.' The promise is greater than the building — God's presence, on condition of obedience. He desires not a house only, but a faithful people.

Solomon finishes the inside with cedar, carved with gourds and open flowers, so no stone shows — all is covered with beauty. The house is adorned with care throughout. What we offer God should be finished well, lovely even in the parts no eye will closely see.

The inner sanctuary, the most holy place, is overlaid entirely with pure gold — even the altar before it. The nearer to God's presence, the more glorious the work. The heart of the house gleams with gold, declaring nothing too precious for the place where God meets His people.

Two great cherubim of olive wood, overlaid with gold, stand in the holy place, their wings outspread across the room. The angelic guardians attend the throne of God. His presence is holy and awesome, surrounded by the worship of heaven even in its earthly picture.

Two great cherubim of olive wood, overlaid with gold, stand in the holy place, their wings outspread across the room. The angelic guardians attend the throne of God. His presence is holy and awesome, surrounded by the worship of heaven even in its earthly picture.

The cherubim, each ten cubits high, stretch their wings until they touch wall to wall and meet in the middle. The whole space is filled with their span. The dwelling of God is not cramped or bare but overshadowed by glory, the place where heaven and earth draw near.

The walls are carved all around with cherubim, palm trees, and open flowers, and even the floor is overlaid with gold. Beauty covers the house from floor to ceiling. God is worthy of loveliness as well as reverence; His house was a garden of gold, recalling Eden's lost glory.

The walls are carved all around with cherubim, palm trees, and open flowers, and even the floor is overlaid with gold. Beauty covers the house from floor to ceiling. God is worthy of loveliness as well as reverence; His house was a garden of gold, recalling Eden's lost glory.

Doors of olive and fir, carved with cherubim and palms and overlaid with gold, guard the entrances to the holy places. Every threshold is made glorious. The way into God's presence is marked with beauty and with the figures of His guardians — sacred, not common ground.

The inner court is built, and after seven years the whole house is finished, exactly as planned. The great work is completed in due time. God's purposes, begun with care, are brought patiently to their end; what He sets His servants to build, He enables them to finish.
